column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=I will introduce my reader to one of these places.
#Text=In one corner,\r
#Text=on the ground, burns a fierce fire, surrounded by innumerable pots\r
#Text=and pans, between which are wooden spits with beef and pork,\r
#Text=simmering and roasting in the most enticing manner.
#Text=An ungainly\r
#Text=wooden framework, with a long broad plank on it, occupies the middle\r
#Text=of the room, and is covered with a cloth whose original colour it\r
#Text=would be an impossibility to determine.
#Text=This is the table at which\r
#Text=the guests sit.
#Text=During the dinner itself the old patriarchal\r
#Text=customs are observed, with this difference, that not only do all the\r
#Text=guests eat out of one dish, but that all the eatables are served up\r
#Text=in one, and one only.
#Text=Beans and rice, potatoes and roast beef,\r
#Text=Paradise apples and onions, etc., etc., lie quietly side by side,\r
#Text=and are devoured in the deepest silence.
#Text=At the end of the repast,\r
#Text=a goblet, filled with wine, or sometimes merely water, is passed\r
#Text=from hand to hand, and after this had gone round, the company begin\r
#Text=to talk.
#Text=In the evening dancing is vigorously pursued to the music\r
#Text=of a guitar; unfortunately, it was Lent during my visit, when all\r
#Text=public amusements are prohibited.
#Text=The people themselves, however,\r
#Text=were not so particular, and were only too ready, for a few reaux, to\r
#Text=go through the Sammaquecca and Refolosa--the national dances of the\r
#Text=country.
#Text=I had soon seen sufficient; the gestures and movements of\r
#Text=the dancers were beyond all description unbecoming, and I could but\r
#Text=pity the children, whose natural modesty cannot fail to be nipped in\r
#Text=the bud by witnessing the performance of these dances.
#Text=I was equally displeased with a remarkable custom prevalent here, in\r
#Text=accordance with which the death of a little child is celebrated by\r
#Text=its parents as a grand festival.
#Text=They name the deceased child an\r
#Text=angelito, (little angel), and adorn it in every possible way.
#Text=Its\r
#Text=eyes are not closed, but, on the contrary, opened as wide as\r
#Text=possible, and its cheeks are painted red; it is then dressed out in\r
#Text=the finest clothes, crowned with flowers, and placed in a little\r
#Text=chair in a kind of niche, which also is ornamented with flowers.
#Text=The relations and neighbours then come and wish the parents joy at\r
#Text=possessing such an angel; and, during the first night, the parents,\r
#Text=relations, and friends execute the wildest dances, and feast in the\r
#Text=most joyous fashion before the angelito.
#Text=I heard that in the\r
#Text=country it was not unusual for the parents to carry the little\r
#Text=coffin to the churchyard themselves, followed by the relations with\r
#Text=the brandy bottle in their hands, and giving vent to their joy in\r
#Text=the most outrageous manner.
#Text=On the 17th of March, Captain Van Wyk Jurianse sent me word that his\r
#Text=ship was ready for sea, and that he should set sail the next\r
#Text=morning.
#Text=The news was very unwelcome to me, as, for the last two\r
#Text=days, I had been suffering from English cholera, which on board\r
#Text=ship, where the patient cannot procure meat broth or any other light\r
#Text=nourishment, and where he is always more exposed to the sudden\r
#Text=changes of the weather than he is on shore, is very apt to be\r
#Text=attended with grave results.
#Text=I did not, however, wish to miss the\r
#Text=opportunity of visiting China, knowing how rarely it occurred, nor\r
#Text=was I desirous of losing the two hundred dollars (40 pounds) already\r
#Text=paid for my passage, and I therefore went on board, trusting in my\r
#Text=good luck, which had never forsaken me on my travels.
#Text=During the first few days, I endeavoured to master my illness by\r
#Text=observing a strict diet, and abstaining from almost everything, but\r
#Text=to no purpose.
#Text=I still continued to suffer, until I luckily thought\r
#Text=of using salt-water baths.
#Text=I took them in a large tub, in which I\r
#Text=remained a quarter of an hour.
#Text=After the second bath, I felt much\r
#Text=better, and after the sixth, I was completely recovered.
#Text=I merely\r
#Text=mention this malady, to which I was very subject in warm climates,\r
#Text=that I may have the opportunity of remarking, that sea-baths or\r
#Text=cooling drinks, such as buttermilk, sour milk, sherbet, orangeade,\r
#Text=etc., are very efficacious remedies.
#Text=The ship in which I made my present voyage, was the Dutch barque\r
#Text=Lootpuit, a fine, strong vessel, quite remarkable for its\r
#Text=cleanliness.
#Text=The table was pretty good, too, with the exception of\r
#Text=a few Dutch dishes, and a superfluity of onions.
#Text=To these, which\r
#Text=played a prominent part in everything that was served up, I really\r
#Text=could not accustom myself, and felt greatly delighted that a large\r
#Text=quantity of this noble production of the vegetable kingdom became\r
#Text=spoilt during the voyage.
#Text=The captain was a polite and kind man, and the mates and sailors\r
#Text=were also civil and obliging.
#Text=In fact, as a general rule, in every\r
#Text=ship that I embarked in, I was far from finding seamen so rough and\r
#Text=uncivil as travellers often represent them to be.
#Text=Their manners are\r
#Text=certainly not the most polished in the world, neither are they\r
#Text=extraordinarily attentive or delicate, but their hearts and\r
#Text=dispositions are mostly good.
#Text=On the 4th of May we put off to sea in a boat, for the purpose of\r
#Text=coasting round to Papara, forty-two miles distant.
#Text=I found the\r
#Text=chief Tati to be a lively old man nearly ninety years of age, who\r
#Text=remembered perfectly the second landing of the celebrated\r
#Text=circumnavigator of the globe, Captain Cook.
#Text=His father was, at that\r
#Text=period, the principal chief, and had concluded a friendly alliance\r
#Text=with Cook, and, according to the custom then prevalent at Tahiti,\r
#Text=had changed names with him.
#Text=Tati enjoys from the French government a yearly pension of 6,000\r
#Text=francs (240 pounds), which, after his death, will fall to his eldest\r
#Text=son.
#Text=He had with him his young wife and five of his sons; the former was\r
#Text=twenty-three years old, and the ages of the latter varied from\r
#Text=twelve to eighteen.
#Text=The children were all the offspring of other\r
#Text=marriages, this being his fifth wife.
#Text=As we had not left Papeiti till nearly noon, and as the sun sets\r
#Text=soon after six o'clock, and the passage between the numberless rocks\r
#Text=is highly dangerous, we landed at Paya (22 miles), where a sixth son\r
#Text=of Tati's ruled as chief.
#Text=The island is intersected in all directions by noble mountains, the\r
#Text=loftiest of which, the Oroena, is 6,200 feet high.
#Text=In the middle of\r
#Text=the island the mountains separate, and a most remarkable mass of\r
#Text=rock raises itself from the midst of them.
#Text=It has the form of a\r
#Text=diadem with a number of points, and it is to this circumstance that\r
#Text=it owes its name.
#Text=Around the mountain range winds a forest girdle,\r
#Text=from four to six hundred paces broad; it is inhabited, and contains\r
#Text=the most delicious fruit.
#Text=Nowhere did I ever eat such bread-fruit,\r
#Text=mangoes, oranges, and guavas, as I did here.
#Text=As for cocoa-nuts, the\r
#Text=natives are so extravagant with them, that they generally merely\r
#Text=drink the water they contain, and then throw away the shell and the\r
#Text=fruit.
#Text=In the mountains and ravines there are a great quantity of\r
#Text=plantains, a kind of banana, which are not commonly eaten, however,\r
#Text=without being roasted.
#Text=The huts of the natives lie scattered here\r
#Text=and there along the shore; it is very seldom that a dozen of these\r
#Text=huts are seen together.
#Text=The bread-fruit is somewhat similar in shape to a water-melon, and\r
#Text=weighs from four to six pounds.
#Text=The outside is green, and rather\r
#Text=rough and thin.
#Text=The natives scrape it with mussel-shells, and then\r
#Text=split the fruit up long ways into two portions, which they roast\r
#Text=between two heated stones.
#Text=The taste is delicious; it is finer than\r
#Text=that of potatoes, and so like bread that the latter may be dispensed\r
#Text=with without any inconvenience.
#Text=The South Sea Islands are the real\r
#Text=home of the fruit.
#Text=It is true that it grows in other parts of the\r
#Text=tropics, but it is very different from that produced here.
#Text=In\r
#Text=Brazil, for instance, where the people call it monkeys' bread, it\r
#Text=weighs from five to thirty pounds, and is full inside of kernels,\r
#Text=which are taken out and eaten when the fruit is roasted.
#Text=These\r
#Text=kernels taste like chestnuts.
#Text=The mango is a fruit resembling an apple, and of the size of a man's\r
#Text=fist; both the rind and the fruit itself are yellow.
#Text=It tastes a\r
#Text=little like turpentine, but loses this taste more and more the riper\r
#Text=it gets.
#Text=This fruit is of the best description; it is full and\r
#Text=juicy, and has a long, broad kernel in the middle.
#Text=The bread and\r
#Text=mango trees grow to a great height and circumference.
#Text=The leaves of\r
#Text=the former are about three feet long, a foot and a-half broad, and\r
#Text=deeply serrated; while those of the latter are not much larger than\r
#Text=the leaves of our own apple-trees.
#Text=On our arrival, and as soon as the stones were heated for our meal,\r
#Text=several of the natives out of the neighbouring huts hastened to\r
#Text=profit by the opportunity to cook their provisions as well, bringing\r
#Text=with them fish, pieces of pork, bread-fruit, plantains, and so on.
#Text=The fish and meat were enveloped in large leaves.
#Text=For our use,\r
#Text=besides bread-fruit and fish, there was a turtle weighing perhaps\r
#Text=more than twenty pounds.
#Text=The repast was held in a hut, to which the\r
#Text=whole neighbourhood also came, and forming themselves into groups a\r
#Text=little on one side of us principal guests, eat the provisions they\r
#Text=had brought with them.
#Text=Each person had a cocoa-nut shell full of\r
#Text=miti before him; into this he first threw every morsel and took it\r
#Text=out again with his hand, and then what remained of the miti was\r
#Text=drunk at the end of the meal.
#Text=We had each of us a fresh cocoa-nut\r
#Text=with a hole bored in it, containing at least a pint of clear, sweet-\r
#Text=tasting water.
#Text=This is erroneously termed by us "Milk," but it only\r
#Text=becomes thick and milky when the cocoa-nut is very stale, in which\r
#Text=condition it is never eaten in these islands.
#Text=For our supper, he gathered a few plantains and laid them on the\r
#Text=fire.
#Text=I profited by the opportunity to dry my clothes, by sitting\r
#Text=down near the fire, and turning first one side towards it, and then\r
#Text=the other.
#Text=Half wet through, and tolerably fatigued, I retired to\r
#Text=my couch of dry leaves immediately after partaking of our scanty\r
#Text=meal.
#Text=It is a fortunate circumstance that in these wild and remote\r
#Text=districts neither men nor beasts afford the slightest grounds for\r
#Text=apprehension; the former are very quiet and peaceably inclined, and,\r
#Text=with the exception of a few wild boars, the latter are not\r
#Text=dangerous.
#Text=The island is especially favoured; it contains no\r
#Text=poisonous or hurtful insects or reptiles.
#Text=It is true there are a\r
#Text=few scorpions, but so small and harmless, that they may be handled\r
#Text=with impunity.
#Text=The mosquitoes alone were the source of very\r
#Text=considerable annoyance, as they are in all southern countries.
#Text=The same evening I witnessed a remarkable mode of catching fish.
#Text=Two boys waded out into the sea, one with a stick, and the other\r
#Text=with a quantity of burning chips.
#Text=The one with the stick drove the\r
#Text=fish between the rocks, and then hit them, the other lighting him in\r
#Text=the meanwhile.
#Text=They were not very fortunate, however.
#Text=The more\r
#Text=common and successful manner of fishing is with nets.
#Text=From all I saw, I must repeat my former assertion, that the Tahitian\r
#Text=people are endowed with none of the more noble sentiments of\r
#Text=humanity, but that their only pleasures are merely animal.
#Text=Nature\r
#Text=herself encourages them to this in an extraordinary manner.
#Text=They\r
#Text=have no need to gain their bread by the sweat of their brow; the\r
#Text=island is most plentifully supplied with beautiful fruit, tubercles\r
#Text=of all descriptions, and tame pigs, so that the people have really\r
#Text=only to gather the fruit and kill the pigs.
#Text=To this circumstance is\r
#Text=to be attributed the difficulty that exists of obtaining any one as\r
#Text=servant or in any other capacity.
#Text=The most wretched journeyman will\r
#Text=not work for less than a dollar a-day; the price for washing a dozen\r
#Text=handkerchiefs, or any other articles, is also a dollar (4s.), not\r
#Text=including soap.
#Text=A native, whom I desired to engage as guide,\r
#Text=demanded a dollar and a half a day.
#Text=The company were, it is true, not very select, but behaved with\r
#Text=great propriety, so that there was nothing which could prevent my\r
#Text=remaining among them.
#Text=Some were playing at dominoes, while others\r
#Text=were extracting most horrible sounds from a sort of mandolin with\r
#Text=three strings; all, however, were smoking, chatting, and drinking\r
#Text=tea, without sugar, from little saucers.
#Text=I, too, had this celestial\r
#Text=drink offered to me on all sides.
#Text=Every Chinese, rich or poor,\r
#Text=drinks neither pure water nor spirituous liquors, but invariably\r
#Text=indulges in weak tea with no sugar.
#Text=Immense plantations of rice, skirted by bananas and other fruit-\r
#Text=trees, extend along the banks of the Pearl stream.
#Text=The trees are\r
#Text=sometimes prettily arranged in alleys, but are planted far less for\r
#Text=ornament than for use.
#Text=Rice always requires a great deal of\r
#Text=moisture, and the trees are planted in order to impart a greater\r
#Text=degree of solidity to the soil, and also to prevent the possibility\r
#Text=of its being washed away by the force of the stream.
#Text=Pretty little\r
#Text=country houses of the genuine Chinese pattern, with their sloping,\r
#Text=pointed, indented roofs, and their coloured tiles inlaid with\r
#Text=different hues, were scattered here and there, under groups of shady\r
#Text=trees, while pagodas (called Tas) of various styles, and from three\r
#Text=to nine stories high, raised their heads on little eminences in the\r
#Text=neighbourhood of the villages, and attracted attention at a great\r
#Text=distance.
#Text=A number of fortifications, which, however, look more\r
#Text=like roofless houses than anything else, protect the stream.
#Text=Very few take their families with them to China, and least of all to\r
#Text=Canton, where both women and children are closely imprisoned in\r
#Text=their houses, which they can only leave in a well-closed litter.
#Text=Besides this, everything is so dear, that living in London is cheap\r
#Text=in comparison.
#Text=Lodgings of six rooms, with a kitchen, cost about\r
#Text=700 or 800 dollars a-year (140 or 160 pounds).
#Text=A man-servant\r
#Text=receives from four to eight dollars a-month, and female servants\r
#Text=nine or ten dollars, as Chinese women will not wait upon a European\r
#Text=unless greatly overpaid.
#Text=In addition to all this, there is a custom\r
#Text=prevalent here, of having a separate person for each branch of\r
#Text=household duty, which renders a large number of servants\r
#Text=indispensable.
#Text=A family of only four persons requires at least eleven or twelve\r
#Text=domestics, if not more.
#Text=In the first place, every member of the\r
#Text=family must have an attendant especially for his or her use; then\r
#Text=there is a man-cook, a number of nursery-maids, and several coolies\r
#Text=for the more menial duties, such as cleaning the rooms, carrying the\r
#Text=wood and water, and so forth.
#Text=In spite of this number of servants,\r
#Text=the attendance is frequently very bad; for, if one or other of them\r
#Text=happens to be out, and his services are required, his master must\r
#Text=wait until he returns, as no servant could ever be prevailed upon to\r
#Text=do another's duty.
#Text=At the head of the whole household is the comprador, who is a kind\r
#Text=of major-domo.
#Text=To his care are confided all the plate, furniture,\r
#Text=linen, and other effects; he engages all the servants, provides for\r
#Text=their board, and anything else they may require, and answers for\r
#Text=their good conduct, deducting, however, two dollars a-month from the\r
#Text=wages of each, in return for his services.
#Text=He makes all the\r
#Text=purchases, and settles all the bills, giving in the sum total at the\r
#Text=end of the month, without descending into the items.
#Text=All the dishes, with the exception of the curry, are prepared after the \r
#Text=English fashion, although the cooks\r
#Text=are Chinese.
#Text=For dessert there is cheese, with fruit; such as pine-\r
#Text=apples, long-yen, mangoes, and lytchi.
#Text=The Chinese affirm that the\r
#Text=latter is the finest fruit in the whole world.
#Text=It is about the size\r
#Text=of a nut, with a brown verrucous outside; the edible part is white\r
#Text=and tender, and the kernel black.
#Text=Long-yen is somewhat smaller, but\r
#Text=is also white and tender, though the taste is rather watery.
#Text=Neither of these fruits struck me as very good.
#Text=I do not think the\r
#Text=pine-apples are so sweet, or possessed of that aromatic fragrance\r
#Text=which distinguishes those raised in our European greenhouses,\r
#Text=although they are much larger.
#Text=Portuguese wines and English beer are the usual drinks--ice, broken\r
#Text=into small pieces, and covered up with a cloth, is offered with\r
#Text=each.
#Text=The ice is rather a costly article, as it has to be brought\r
#Text=from North America.
#Text=In the evening, tea is served up.
#Text=During meal-times, a large punkah is employed to diffuse an\r
#Text=agreeable degree of coolness through the apartment.
#Text=The punkah is a\r
#Text=large frame, from eight to ten feet long, and three feet high,\r
#Text=covered with white Indian cloth, and fastened to the ceiling.
#Text=A\r
#Text=rope communicates, through the wall, like a bell-pull, with the next\r
#Text=room, or the ground floor, where a servant is stationed to keep it\r
#Text=constantly in motion, and thus maintain a pleasing draught.
#Text=It is true,\r
#Text=the Chinese are not too particular in their food; they eat dogs,\r
#Text=cats, mice, and rats, the intestines of birds, and the blood of\r
#Text=every animal, and I was even assured that caterpillars and worms\r
#Text=formed part of their diet.
#Text=Their principal dish, however, is rice,\r
#Text=which is not only employed by them in the composition of their\r
#Text=various dishes, but supplies the place of bread.
#Text=It is exceedingly\r
#Text=cheap; the pekul, which is equal to 124 lbs.
#Text=English avoirdupois,\r
#Text=costing from one dollar and three-quarters to two dollars and a\r
#Text=half.
#Text=The cheapest mode of living is on board a boat.
#Text=The husband goes on\r
#Text=shore to his work, and leaves his wife to make a trifle by ferrying\r
#Text=persons over, or letting out the boat to pleasure parties.
#Text=One half\r
#Text=the boat belongs to the family themselves, and the other half to the\r
#Text=persons to whom they let it; and although there is not much room,\r
#Text=the whole boat measuring scarcely twenty-five feet in length, the\r
#Text=greatest order and cleanliness is everywhere apparent, as each\r
#Text=single plank on board is thoroughly scrubbed and washed every\r
#Text=morning.
#Text=Great ingenuity is displayed in turning every inch of\r
#Text=space on board these small craft to advantage, and the dexterity is\r
#Text=actually pushed so far as to find room for a tiny domestic altar.
#Text=During the day all the cookery and washing is done, and though at\r
#Text=the latter process there is no want of little children, the\r
#Text=temporary tenant of the boat does not suffer the least annoyance;\r
#Text=nothing offensive meets his eye; and, at the most, he merely hears\r
#Text=at rare intervals the whining voice of some poor little wretch.
#Text=The\r
#Text=youngest child is generally tied on its mother's back while she\r
#Text=steers; the elder children, too, have sometimes similar burdens, but\r
#Text=jump and climb about without the least consideration for them.
#Text=It\r
#Text=has often grieved me to the heart to see the head of an infant\r
#Text=scarcely born, thrown from one side to the other with each movement\r
#Text=of the child that was carrying it, or the sun darting so fiercely on\r
#Text=the poor little creature, who was completely exposed to its rays,\r
#Text=that it could hardly open its eyes.
#Text=For those who have not been\r
#Text=themselves witnesses of the fact, it is almost impossible to form an\r
#Text=idea of the indigence and poverty of a Chinese boat-family.
#Text=We here saw an opium-eater, lying stretched out upon a mat on the\r
#Text=floor.
#Text=At his side was a cup of tea, with some fruit and a little\r
#Text=lamp, besides several pipes, with bowls that were smaller than a\r
#Text=thimble.
#Text=On our entrance, he was just inhaling the intoxicating\r
#Text=smoke from one of them.
#Text=It is said that some of the Chinese opium\r
#Text=smokers consume from twenty to thirty grains a-day.
#Text=As he was not\r
#Text=altogether unconscious of our presence, he managed to raise himself,\r
#Text=laid by his pipe, and dragged himself to a chair.
#Text=His eyes were\r
#Text=fixed and staring, and his face deadly pale, presenting altogether a\r
#Text=most pitiable and wretched spectacle.
#Text=The villages through which we passed presented an animated\r
#Text=appearance, but appeared poor and dirty.
#Text=We were often obliged to\r
#Text=hold our noses in passing through the lanes and squares, and very\r
#Text=frequently would fain have closed our eyes as well, to avoid the\r
#Text=disgusting sight of people covered with eruptions of the skin,\r
#Text=tumours, and boils.
#Text=In all the villages I saw poultry and swine in great numbers, but\r
#Text=not more than three horses and a buffalo-cow; both the horses and\r
#Text=the cow were of an extremely small breed.
#Text=The company at our table consisted not only of the passengers, of\r
#Text=whom there were three besides myself, but of the cooks and waiters\r
#Text=of the first-class places, as well as of the butcher; or, in a word,\r
#Text=of every one of the attendants who chose to take "pot-luck" with us.
#Text=As for any etiquette in the article of costume, that was entirely\r
#Text=out of the question.
#Text=Sometimes one of the company would appear\r
#Text=without either coat or jacket; the butcher was generally oblivious\r
#Text=of his shoes and stockings; and it was really necessary to be\r
#Text=endowed with a ravenous appetite to be enabled to eat anything with\r
#Text=such a set.
#Text=The table-cloth was full of stains, and, in lieu of a napkin, each\r
#Text=guest was at liberty to use his handkerchief.
#Text=The knives and forks\r
#Text=had white and black horn handles, with notched blades, and broken\r
#Text=prongs.
#Text=On the first day we had no spoons at all; on the second we\r
#Text=had one between us, and this one was placed on the table in solitary\r
#Text=grandeur during the entire voyage.
#Text=There were only two glasses, and\r
#Text=those of the most ordinary description, which circulated from mouth\r
#Text=to mouth; as I was a female, instead of my turn of the glasses, I\r
#Text=had, as a peculiar mark of distinction, an old tea-cup with the\r
#Text=handle knocked off.
#Text=On one occasion, during tea, the head cook was in unusually good\r
#Text=humour, and remarked, "I spare no possible pains to provide for you.

column3:
_	

#Text=Two of the passengers, Englishmen,\r
#Text=replied, No, that's true!"

column1: #Text=The third, who was a Portuguese, did\r
#Text=not understand the importance of the assertion.
#Text=As a native of\r
#Text=Germany, not possessing the patriotic feeling of an English subject\r
#Text=in the matter, I should have replied very differently had I not been\r
#Text=a women, and if, by so replying, I could have effected a change for\r
#Text=the better.
#Text=Some fruit and biscuits of various descriptions, with a very small\r
#Text=quantity of boiled rice, were placed upon the altar of the Goddess\r
#Text=of Mercy.
#Text=These are renewed every evening, and whatever the goddess\r
#Text=may leave is the perquisite of the bonzes.
#Text=On the same altar lay\r
#Text=pretty little wooden counters cut in an oval shape, which the\r
#Text=Chinese toss up in the air; it is held to be a sign of ill-luck if\r
#Text=they fall upon the reverse side, but if they fall upon the other,\r
#Text=this is believed to betoken good fortune.
#Text=The worthy people are in\r
#Text=the habit of tossing them up until they fall as desired.
#Text=The plantations that we now saw, and which we reached about 10\r
#Text=o'clock, were cultivated by Chinese.
#Text=In addition to their huts of\r
#Text=leaves, they had erected a small temple, where they invited us to\r
#Text=alight.
#Text=We immediately spread out upon the altar some refreshments,\r
#Text=which Madame Behu, like a good housewife, had given us; but, instead\r
#Text=of imitating the Chinese, and sacrificing them to the gods, we were\r
#Text=wicked enough to devour them ravenously ourselves.
#Text=When we had satisfied our hunger, we skinned the serpent and then\r
#Text=made a present of it to the Chinese; but they gave us to understand\r
#Text=that they would not touch it, at which I was greatly surprised,\r
#Text=since they will generally eat anything.
#Text=I was afterwards convinced\r
#Text=that this was all pretence, for on returning some hours later from\r
#Text=our hunting excursion and going into one of their huts, we found\r
#Text=them all seated round a large dish in which were pieces of roast\r
#Text=meat of the peculiar round shape of the serpent.
#Text=They wanted to\r
#Text=hide the dish in a great hurry, but I entered very quickly and gave\r
#Text=them some money to be allowed to taste it.
#Text=I found the flesh\r
#Text=particularly tender and delicate, even more tender than that of a\r
#Text=chicken.
#Text=After this, boiled fowls, ducks, pork, fruit, all kinds of pastry,\r
#Text=and a dozen cups full of tea, together with the tea-pot, were placed\r
#Text=in two rows upon the grave, and six painted wax tapers lighted and\r
#Text=stuck in the ground near the refreshments.
#Text=During all this time,\r
#Text=immense heaps of gold and silver paper were set fire to and\r
#Text=consumed.
#Text=CHAPTER X.
#Text=THE EAST INDIES--CEYLON.
#Text=The treatment I experienced on board this vessel was, it is true, a\r
#Text=little different from that on board the other, although it was\r
#Text=nearly as bad.
#Text=There were four of us in the second cabin; {128} we\r
#Text=dined alone, and had a mulatto servant to attend upon us.
#Text=Unfortunately, he was afflicted with elephantiasis, and his\r
#Text=appearance did not at all tend to whet the edge of our appetites.
#Text=But if the scenery around us was not remarkable, the spectacle on\r
#Text=board the vessel itself was highly interesting.
#Text=The crew was\r
#Text=composed of seventy-nine persons, comprising Chinese, Malays,\r
#Text=Cingalese, Bengalese, Hindostanese, and Europeans.
#Text=As a general\r
#Text=rule, those of each country generally took their meals separately\r
#Text=with their own countrymen.
#Text=They all had immense plates of rice, and\r
#Text=little bowls full of curry; a few pieces of dried fish supplied the\r
#Text=place of bread.
#Text=They poured the curry over the rice, and mixing the\r
#Text=whole together with their hands, made it into small balls which they\r
#Text=put into their mouths with a small piece of fish; about half their\r
#Text=food used generally to fall back again into their plates.
#Text=In the afternoon I took a walk to one of the numerous cinnamon\r
#Text=plantations round Colombo.
#Text=The cinnamon tree or bush is planted in\r
#Text=rows; it attains at most a height of nine feet, and bears a white,\r
#Text=scentless blossom.
#Text=From the fruit, which is smaller than an acorn,\r
#Text=oil is obtained by crushing and boiling it; the oil then disengages\r
#Text=itself and floats on the top of the water.
#Text=It is mixed with cocoa-\r
#Text=oil and used for burning.
#Text=There are two cinnamon harvests in the course of the year.
#Text=The\r
#Text=first and principal one takes place from April to July, and the\r
#Text=second from November to January.
#Text=The rind is peeled from the\r
#Text=branches by means of knives, and then dried in the sun; this gives\r
#Text=it a yellowish or brownish tint.
#Text=The best cinnamon is a light\r
#Text=yellow, and not thicker than pasteboard.
#Text=The essential oil of cinnamon, used in medicine, is extracted from\r
#Text=the plant itself, which is placed in a vessel full of water, and\r
#Text=left to steep for eight to ten days.
#Text=The whole mass is then\r
#Text=transferred to a retort and distilled over a slow fire.
#Text=In a short\r
#Text=time, on the surface of the water thus distilled a quantity of oil\r
#Text=collects, and this is then skimmed off with the greatest care.
#Text=In the animal kingdom, besides the elephants, I was much struck by\r
#Text=the number and tameness of the ravens of Ceylon.
#Text=In every small\r
#Text=town and village may be seen multitudes of these birds, that come up\r
#Text=to the very doors and windows and pick up everything.
#Text=They play the\r
#Text=part of scavengers here, just as dogs do in Turkey.
#Text=The horned\r
#Text=cattle are rather small, with humps between the shoulder-blades;\r
#Text=these humps consist of flesh and are considered a great dainty.
#Text=In Colombo and Pointe de Galle there are likewise a great many large\r
#Text=white buffaloes, belonging to the English government, and imported\r
#Text=from Bengal.
#Text=They are employed in drawing heavy loads.
#Text=Under the head of fruit, I may mention the pine-apple as being\r
#Text=particularly large and good.
#Text=CHAPTER XI.
#Text=MADRAS AND CALCUTTA.
#Text=The companions of the prince took their meals with the servants.
#Text=A\r
#Text=carpet was spread out upon the deck, and two large dishes, one\r
#Text=containing boiled fowls, and the other pillau, placed upon it; the\r
#Text=company used their hands for knives and forks.
#Text=A speculative baker of Madras had come out in the first boat that\r
#Text=reached the steamer with ice and biscuits for sale, which he\r
#Text=disposed of very much to his profit.
#Text=When I reached Moasa, every one hastened to offer me their services--\r
#Text=for I travelled with the king's people, and in this part of the\r
#Text=country a European woman is a rarity.
#Text=They brought me wood, milk,\r
#Text=and eggs.
#Text=My table was always rather frugally furnished:  at the\r
#Text=best I had rice boiled in milk or some eggs, but generally only\r
#Text=rice, with water and salt.
#Text=A leathern vessel for water, a little\r
#Text=saucepan for boiling in, a handful of salt, and some rice and bread,\r
#Text=were all that I took with me.
#Text=The maid informed me, partly by signs, partly by words, that I had\r
#Text=been expected since noon; that a palanquin stood ready for me, and\r
#Text=that I could sleep as well in it as in the tent.
#Text=I was rejoiced at\r
#Text=this, and again started on my journey at 11 o'clock at night.
#Text=The\r
#Text=country was indeed, as I knew, infested with tigers, but as several\r
#Text=torch-bearers accompanied us, and the tigers are sworn enemies of\r
#Text=light, I could composedly continue my uninterrupted sleep.
#Text=About 3\r
#Text=o'clock in the morning, I was set down again in a tent, which was\r
#Text=prepared for my reception, and furnished with every convenience.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=16th February.
#Text=This morning I made the acquaintance of the amiable\r
#Text=family of the Burdons.
#Text=They have seven children, whom they educate\r
#Text=chiefly themselves.
#Text=They live very pleasantly and comfortably,\r
#Text=although they are wholly thrown on their own resources for\r
#Text=amusement, as there are, with the exception of Dr.
#Text=Rolland, no\r
#Text=Europeans in Kottah.
#Text=It is only very rarely that they are visited\r
#Text=by officers who may be passing through, and I was the first European\r
#Text=female Mrs.
#Text=Burdon had seen for four years.
#Text=I passed the most delightful day in this family circle.
#Text=I was not a\r
#Text=little astonished to find here all the conveniences of a well-\r
#Text=regulated house; and I must take this opportunity of describing, in\r
#Text=few words, the mode of travelling adopted by the English officers\r
#Text=and officials in India.
#Text=In the first place, they have tents which are so large, that they\r
#Text=contain two or three rooms; one which I saw was worth more than 800\r
#Text=rupees (80 pounds).
#Text=They take with them corresponding furniture,\r
#Text=from a footstool to the most elegant divan; in fact, nearly the\r
#Text=whole of the house and cooking utensils.
#Text=They have also a multitude\r
#Text=of servants, every one of whom has his particular occupation, which\r
#Text=he understands exceedingly well.
#Text=The travellers, after passing the\r
#Text=night in their beds, about 3 o'clock in the morning either lie or\r
#Text=sit in easy palanquins, or mount on horseback, and after four or\r
#Text=five hours' ride, dismount, and partake of a hot breakfast under\r
#Text=tents.
#Text=They have every household accommodation, carry on their\r
#Text=ordinary occupations, take their meals at their usual hours, and\r
#Text=are, in fact, entirely at home.
#Text=The cook always proceeds on his journey at night.
#Text=As soon as the\r
#Text=tents are vacated, they are taken down and quickly removed, and as\r
#Text=quickly re-erected:  there is no scarcity of hands or of beasts of\r
#Text=burden.
#Text=In the most cultivated countries of Europe, people do not\r
#Text=travel with so much luxury and ease as in India.
#Text=In the evening, I was obliged to take my departure again.
#Text=Captain\r
#Text=Burdon very kindly offered me the use of his palanquin and the\r
#Text=necessary bearers as far as Indos, but I pitied the people too much,\r
#Text=and declared that I did not find travelling on camels unpleasant;\r
#Text=that in fact, on account of the open view, that mode was to be\r
#Text=preferred to palanquins.
#Text=However, on account of my little\r
#Text=portmanteau, I took a third camel.
#Text=I left the sepoys behind here.
#Text=This evening we went eight miles towards the little town Patan.
#Text=After tiffen (lunch) I was to see the town, and be presented at\r
#Text=court.
#Text=I employed the intermediate time in visiting Mr. and Mrs.
#Text=Naher.
#Text=The latter, who was also a German, was moved even to tears\r
#Text=when she saw me:  for fifteen years she had not spoken with a\r
#Text=fellow-countrywoman.
#Text=We were offered sweetmeats and fruits, sprinkled with rosewater, and\r
#Text=some attar of roses was put upon our handkerchiefs.
#Text=After some time\r
#Text=areca nuts and betel leaves were brought on silver plates, which the\r
#Text=queen herself handed to us; this is a sign that the audience is at\r
#Text=an end, and visitors cannot leave until it is made.
#Text=Before we got\r
#Text=up to go, large wreaths of jasmine were hung round our necks, and\r
#Text=small ones round our wrists.
#Text=Fruits and sweetmeats were also sent\r
#Text=home to us.
#Text=The queen had given the mundsch directions to conduct us round the\r
#Text=whole of the palace.
#Text=It is not very large, and the rooms, with the\r
#Text=exception of the reception-saloon, are very simple, and almost\r
#Text=without furniture; in each, cushions covered with white muslin lie\r
#Text=upon the floor.
#Text=The mundsch was good enough to show me the mode adopted for making\r
#Text=ice.
#Text=The proper time for this is during the months of December and\r
#Text=January; although, even in the month of February, the nights, and\r
#Text=especially the early hours of the morning before sun-rise, are so\r
#Text=cold, that small quantities of water are covered with a thin sheet\r
#Text=of ice.
#Text=For this purpose, either shallow pits are dug in earth rich\r
#Text=in saltpetre, {212b} and small shallow dishes of burnt porous clay\r
#Text=are filled with water, and placed in these pits, or when the soil\r
#Text=does not contain any saltpetre, the highest terraces on the houses\r
#Text=are covered with straw, and the little dishes of water are placed up\r
#Text=there.
#Text=The thin crusts of ice thus obtained are broken into small\r
#Text=pieces, a little water is poured over them, and the whole is put\r
#Text=into the ice-houses, which are also lined with straw.
#Text=This mode of\r
#Text=obtaining ice is already practised in Benares.
#Text=After I had wandered about here for some hours, and had seen enough\r
#Text=of each of the temples, I was led back to one of them, and saw there\r
#Text=a small table well covered with eatables and drinkables, inviting me\r
#Text=to a welcome meal.
#Text=Captain Gill had been so kind as to send after\r
#Text=me a choice tiffen, together with table and chairs, into this\r
#Text=wilderness.
#Text=Thus refreshed and invigorated, I did not find the\r
#Text=return fatiguing.
#Text=The house in which Captain Gill lives at Adjunta\r
#Text=is very remarkably situated:  a pleasant little garden, with flowers\r
#Text=and shrubs, surrounds the front, which commands a view of a fine\r
#Text=plain, while the back stands upon the edge of a most fearful\r
#Text=precipice, over which the dizzy glance loses itself among steep\r
#Text=crags and terrible gorges and chasms.
#Text=The steamer "Sir Charles Forbes" (forty horse-power, Captain\r
#Text=Lichfield) had only two cabins, a small and a large one.
#Text=The former\r
#Text=had already been engaged for some time by an Englishman, Mr.
#Text=Ross;\r
#Text=the latter was bespoken by some rich Persians for their wives and\r
#Text=children.
#Text=I was, therefore, obliged to content myself with a place\r
#Text=upon deck; however, I took my meals at the captain's table, who\r
#Text=showed me the most extreme attention and kindness during the whole\r
#Text=voyage.
#Text=The little vessel was, in the fullest sense of the word, overloaded\r
#Text=with people; the crew alone numbered forty-five; in addition to that\r
#Text=there were 124 passengers, chiefly Persians, Mahomedans, and Arabs.
#Text=Mr.
#Text=Ross and myself were the only Europeans.
#Text=When this crowd of\r
#Text=persons were collected, there was not the smallest clear space on\r
#Text=the deck; to get from one place to another it was necessary to climb\r
#Text=over innumerable chests and boxes, and at the same time to use great\r
#Text=caution not to tread upon the heads or feet of the people.
#Text=In such critical circumstances I looked about immediately to see\r
#Text=where I could possibly secure a good place.
#Text=I found what I sought,\r
#Text=and was the most fortunate of all the passengers, more so than even\r
#Text=Mr.
#Text=Ross, who could not sleep any night in his cabin on account of\r
#Text=the heat and insects.
#Text=My eye fell upon the under part of the\r
#Text=captain's dinner-table, which was fixed upon the stern deck; I took\r
#Text=possession of this place, threw my mantle round me, so that I had a\r
#Text=pretty secure position, and no cause to fear that I should have my\r
#Text=hands, feet, or indeed my head trodden upon.
#Text=I was somewhat unwell when I left Bombay, and on the second day of\r
#Text=the voyage a slight attack of bilious fever came on.
#Text=I had to\r
#Text=contend with this for five days.
#Text=I crept painfully from my asylum\r
#Text=at meal times to make way for the feet of the people at table.
#Text=I\r
#Text=did not take any medicine (I carried none with me), but trusted to\r
#Text=Providence and my good constitution.
#Text=There are considerable quantities of sea-weeds and molluscae in the\r
#Text=Persian Gulf; the latter had many fibres, were of a milk-white\r
#Text=colour, and resembled a forest agaric in form; others had a\r
#Text=glistening rose colour with small yellow spots.
#Text=Conger eels of two\r
#Text=or three feet in length were not uncommon.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=8th May.
#Text=The town of Buschir is situated on a plain six miles from\r
#Text=the mountains, whose highest peak, called by the Persians Hormutsch,\r
#Text=by the English Halala, is 5,000 feet high.
#Text=The town contains 15,000 inhabitants, and has the best harbour in\r
#Text=Persia; but its appearance is very dirty and ugly.
#Text=The houses stand quite close together, so that it is easy to pass\r
#Text=from one to the other over the terraces, and it requires no great\r
#Text=exertion to run over the roofs, as the terraces are enclosed only by\r
#Text=walls one or two feet high.
#Text=Upon some houses, square chambers\r
#Text=(called wind-catchers), fifteen or twenty feet high, are erected,\r
#Text=which can be opened above and at the sides, and serve to intercept\r
#Text=the wind and lead it into the apartments.
#Text=The women here cover up their faces to such a degree that I cannot\r
#Text=imagine how they find their way about.
#Text=Even the smallest girls\r
#Text=imitate this foolish custom.
#Text=There is also no lack of nose-rings,\r
#Text=bracelets, sandals, etc.; but they do not wear nearly so many as the\r
#Text=Hindoos.
#Text=The men are all armed; even in the house they carry\r
#Text=daggers or knives, and besides these, pistols in the streets.
#Text=We remained two days in Buschir, where I was very well received by\r
#Text=Lieutenant Hennelt, the resident.
#Text=I would gladly have left the ship here to visit the ruins of\r
#Text=Persepolis, and travel by land from thence to Shiraz, Ispahan,\r
#Text=Teheran, and so onwards; but serious disturbances had broken out in\r
#Text=these districts, and numerous hordes of robbers carried on their\r
#Text=depredations.
#Text=I was in consequence compelled to alter my plan, and\r
#Text=to go straight on to Baghdad.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=10th May.
#Text=In the afternoon we left Buschir.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=11th May.
#Text=Today I had the gratification of seeing and sailing on\r
#Text=one of the most celebrated rivers in the world, the Schatel-Arab\r
#Text=(river of the Arabs), which is formed by the junction of the\r
#Text=Euphrates, Tigris, and Kaurun, and whose mouth resembles an arm of\r
#Text=the sea.
#Text=The Schatel-Arab retains its name as far as the delta of\r
#Text=the Tigris and Euphrates.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=12th May.
#Text=We left the sea and the mountains behind at the same\r
#Text=time, and on both shores immense plains opened before us whose\r
#Text=boundaries were lost in the distance.
#Text=Twenty miles below Bassora we turned off into the Kaurun to set down\r
#Text=some passengers at the little town of Mahambrah, which lies near the\r
#Text=entrance of that river.
#Text=We immediately turned back again, and the\r
#Text=captain brought the vessel round in the narrow space in an\r
#Text=exceedingly clever way.
#Text=This proceeding caused the uninitiated some\r
#Text=anxiety; we expected every moment to see either the head or stern\r
#Text=run a-ground, but it succeeded well beyond all measure.
#Text=The whole\r
#Text=population of the town was assembled on the shore; they had never\r
#Text=before seen a steamer, and took the most lively interest in the bold\r
#Text=and hazardous enterprise.
#Text=About six years ago, the town Mahambrah experienced a terrible\r
#Text=catastrophe; it was at that time under Turkish rule, and was\r
#Text=surprised and plundered by the Persians; nearly all the inhabitants,\r
#Text=amounting to 5,000, were put to death.
#Text=Since that period it has\r
#Text=been retained by the Persians.
#Text=Towards noon we arrived at Bassora.
#Text=Nothing is visible from the\r
#Text=river but some fortified works and large forests of date-trees,\r
#Text=behind which the town is situated far inland.
#Text=The journey from Bombay to this place had occupied eighteen days, in\r
#Text=consequence of the unfavourable monsoon, and was one of the most\r
#Text=unpleasant voyages which I ever made.
#Text=Always upon deck in the midst\r
#Text=of a dense crowd of people, with a heat which at noon time rose to\r
#Text=99 degrees 5' Fah., even under the shade of a tent.
#Text=I was only once\r
#Text=able to change my linen and dress at Buschir, which was the more\r
#Text=annoying as one could not prevent the accumulation of vermin.
#Text=I\r
#Text=longed for a refreshing and purifying bath.
#Text=Bassora, one of the largest towns of Mesopotamia, has among its\r
#Text=inhabitants only a single European.
#Text=I had a letter to the English\r
#Text=agent, an Armenian named Barseige, whose hospitality I was compelled\r
#Text=to claim, as there was no hotel.
#Text=Captain Lichfield presented my\r
#Text=letter to him and made known my request, but the polite man refused\r
#Text=to grant it.
#Text=The good captain offered me accommodation on board his\r
#Text=ship, so that I was provided for for the present.
#Text=The landing of the Persian women presented a most laughable\r
#Text=spectacle:  if they had been beauties of the highest order, or\r
#Text=princesses from the sultan's harem, there could not have been more\r
#Text=care taken to conceal them from the possibility of being seen by\r
#Text=men.
#Text=I was indebted to my sex for the few glimpses which I caught of them\r
#Text=in the cabin; but among the whole eighteen women I did not see a\r
#Text=single good-looking one.
#Text=Their husbands placed themselves in two\r
#Text=rows from the cabin to the ship's ladder, holding large cloths\r
#Text=stretched before them, and forming in this way a kind of opaque\r
#Text=moveable wall on both sides.
#Text=Presently the women came out of the\r
#Text=cabin; they were so covered with large wrappers that they had to be\r
#Text=led as if they were blind.
#Text=They stood close together between the\r
#Text=walls, and waited until the whole were assembled, when the entire\r
#Text=party, namely, the moveable wall and the beauties concealed behind\r
#Text=it, proceeded step by step.
#Text=The scrambling over the narrow ship's\r
#Text=ladders was truly pitiable; first one stumbled, and then another.
#Text=The landing occupied more than an hour.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=13th May.
#Text=The captain brought me word that a German missionary was\r
#Text=accidentally at Bassora, who had a dwelling with several rooms, and\r
#Text=could probably give me shelter.
#Text=I went to him immediately, and he\r
#Text=was so obliging as to provide me with a room in which, at the same\r
#Text=time, I found a fireplace.
#Text=I took leave of the good captain with\r
#Text=sincere regret.
#Text=I shall never forget his friendliness and\r
#Text=attentions.
#Text=He was a truly good-hearted man, and yet the\r
#Text=unfortunate crew, mostly Hindoos and negroes, were treated worse on\r
#Text=board his ship than I had observed elsewhere.
#Text=This was the fault of\r
#Text=the two mates, who accompanied nearly every word with pushes and\r
#Text=blows of the fist.
#Text=In Muscat three of the poor fellows ran away.
#Text=The Christian Europeans excel the pagan Hindoos and Musselmen in\r
#Text=learning and science; might they not also at least equal the latter\r
#Text=in kindness and humanity?
#Text=A small English war-steamer was expected at Bassora in the course of\r
#Text=a few days, which carried letters and dispatches between this place\r
#Text=and Baghdad, and whose captain was so good as to take European\r
#Text=travellers (of whom there are not many that lose themselves here)\r
#Text=with him.
#Text=I availed myself of the few days of my stay to look about the town,\r
#Text=and see what still remains of its ancient celebrity.
#Text=Bassora, or Bassra, was founded in the reign of the Caliph Omar, in\r
#Text=the year 656.
#Text=Sometimes under Turkish, sometimes under Persian\r
#Text=dominion, it was at last permanently placed under the latter power.
#Text=There are no vestiges of antiquity remaining; neither ruins of\r
#Text=handsome mosques nor caravansaries.
#Text=The fortified walls are much\r
#Text=dilapidated, the houses of the town small and unattractive, the\r
#Text=streets crooked, narrow, and dirty.
#Text=The bazaar, which consists of\r
#Text=covered galleries with wretched stalls, cannot show a single good\r
#Text=stock of goods, although Bassora is the principal emporium and\r
#Text=trading port for the Indian wares imported into Turkey.
#Text=There are\r
#Text=several coffee-stalls and a second-rate caravansary in the bazaar.
#Text=A large open space, not very remarkable for cleanliness, serves in\r
#Text=the day as a corn-market; and in the evening several hundred guests\r
#Text=are to be seen seated before a large coffee-stall, drinking coffee\r
#Text=and smoking nargillies.
#Text=Modern ruins are abundant in Bassora, the result of the plague which\r
#Text=in the year 1832 carried off nearly one half of the inhabitants.
#Text=Numbers of streets and squares consist only of forsaken and decaying\r
#Text=houses.
#Text=Where, a few years back, men were busily engaged in trade,\r
#Text=there is now nothing left but ruins and rubbish and weeds, and palms\r
#Text=grow between crumbling walls.
#Text=The position of Bassora is said to be particularly unhealthy:  the\r
#Text=plain surrounding it is intersected at one extremity with numerous\r
#Text=ditches filled with mud and filth, which give off noxious\r
#Text=exhalations, at the other it is covered with forests of date trees,\r
#Text=which hinders the current of air.
#Text=The heat is so great here, that\r
#Text=nearly every house is furnished with an apartment, which lies\r
#Text=several feet below the level of the street, and has windows only in\r
#Text=the high arches.
#Text=People live in these rooms during the day.
#Text=The inhabitants consist for the most part of Arabs; the rest are\r
#Text=Persians, Turks, and Armenians.
#Text=There are no Europeans.
#Text=I was\r
#Text=advised to wrap myself in a large cloth and wear a veil when I went\r
#Text=out; the former I did, but I could not endure the veil in the\r
#Text=excessive heat, and went with my face uncovered.
#Text=The cloth (isar) I\r
#Text=carried so clumsily that my European clothes were always visible;\r
#Text=nevertheless I was not annoyed by any one.
#Text=On the 16th of May, the steamer Nitocris arrived.
#Text=It was small\r
#Text=(forty horse power), but very handsome and clean; the captain, Mr.
#Text=Johns, declared himself ready to take me, and the first officer, Mr.
#Text=Holland, gave up his cabin to me.
#Text=They would not take any\r
#Text=compensation either for passage or board.
#Text=The journey from Bassora to Baghdad would have been very fatiguing\r
#Text=and inconvenient if I had not met with this opportunity.
#Text=With a\r
#Text=boat it would have required forty or fifty days, as the distance is\r
#Text=500 English miles, and the boat must have been for greater part of\r
#Text=the distance drawn by men.
#Text=The distance by land amounts to 390\r
#Text=miles; but the road is through deserts, which are inhabited by\r
#Text=nomadic tribes of Bedouins, and over-run with hordes of robbers,\r
#Text=whose protection must be purchased at a high price.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=17th May.
#Text=We weighed anchor in the morning at 11 o'clock, and\r
#Text=availed ourselves of the current which extends 120 miles up the\r
#Text=stream.
#Text=In the afternoon we reached the point Korne, also called the Delta\r
#Text=(fifty miles from Bassora).
#Text=The Tigris and Euphrates join here.
#Text=Both rivers are equally large, and as it could not, probably, be\r
#Text=decided which name should be retained, both were given up, and that\r
#Text=of Schatel-Arab adopted.
#Text=Many learned writers attempt to give increased importance to this\r
#Text=place, by endeavouring to prove by indubitable evidence that the\r
#Text=garden of Eden was situated here.
#Text=If this was the case, our worthy\r
#Text=progenitor made a long journey after he was driven out of Paradise,\r
#Text=to reach Adam's Peak in Ceylon.
#Text=We now entered the Tigris.
#Text=For a distance of three miles further,\r
#Text=we were gratified by the sight of beautiful forests of date-trees,\r
#Text=which we had already enjoyed, almost without intermission, from the\r
#Text=mouth of the Schatel-Arab; they now suddenly terminated.
#Text=Both sides\r
#Text=of the river were still covered with a rich vegetation, and\r
#Text=beautiful orchards, alternated with extended plots of grass, which\r
#Text=were partially covered with bushes or shrub-like trees.
#Text=This\r
#Text=fruitfulness, however, is said to extend only a few miles inland:\r
#Text=more distant from the river the country is a barren wilderness.
#Text=We saw in several places large tribes of Bedouins, who had pitched\r
#Text=their tents in long rows, for the most part close to the banks.
#Text=Some of these hordes had large closely-covered tents; others again\r
#Text=had merely a straw mat, a cloth, or some skins stretched on a pair\r
#Text=of poles, scarcely protecting the heads of those lying under them\r
#Text=from the burning rays of the sun.
#Text=In winter, when the temperature\r
#Text=frequently falls to freezing point, they have the same dwellings and\r
#Text=clothing as in summer:  the mortality among them is then very great.
#Text=These people have a wild appearance, and their clothing consists of\r
#Text=only a dark-brown mantle.
#Text=The men have a part of this drawn between\r
#Text=the legs, and another part hung round them; the women completely\r
#Text=envelop themselves in it; the children very commonly go quite naked\r
#Text=until the twelfth year.
#Text=The colour of their skin is a dark brown,\r
#Text=the face slightly tattooed:  both the men and women braid their hair\r
#Text=into four plaits, which hang down upon the back of the head and\r
#Text=temples.
#Text=The weapons of the men are stout knotted sticks; the women\r
#Text=are fond of adorning themselves with glass beads, mussel-shells, and\r
#Text=coloured rags; they also wear large nose-rings.
#Text=They are all divided into tribes, and are under the dominion of the\r
#Text=Porte, to whom they pay tribute; but they acknowledge allegiance\r
#Text=only to the sheikh elected by themselves, many of whom have forty or\r
#Text=fifty thousand tents under their control.
#Text=Those tribes who\r
#Text=cultivate land have fixed dwellings; the pastoral tribes are\r
#Text=nomadic.
#Text=Half-way between Bassora and Baghdad, the lofty mountain chain of\r
#Text=Luristan becomes visible.
#Text=When the atmosphere is clear, the\r
#Text=summits, 10,000 feet high, and covered with perpetual snow, may be\r
#Text=seen.
#Text=Every step in advance leads to the scene of the great deeds of\r
#Text=Cambyses, Cyrus, Alexander, etc.:  every spot of ground has\r
#Text=historical associations.
#Text=The country is the same; but what has\r
#Text=become of its towns and its powerful empires?
#Text=Ruined walls and\r
#Text=heaps of earth and rubbish are the only remains of the most\r
#Text=beautiful cities; and where firmly established empires formerly\r
#Text=existed, are barren steppes overrun by robber hordes.
#Text=The Arabs engaged in agriculture are themselves exposed to the\r
#Text=depredations of their nomadic countrymen, especially in harvest\r
#Text=time.
#Text=In order to avoid this evil as much as possible, they bring\r
#Text=their crops into small fortified places, of which I observed many\r
#Text=between Bassora and Baghdad.
#Text=We took in wood several times during the passage, and on these\r
#Text=occasions I could approach the inhabitants without fear, as they\r
#Text=were inspired with respect for the well-manned and armed vessel.
#Text=In\r
#Text=one instance, I was led far into the underwood in pursuit of some\r
#Text=beautiful insects, when I found myself on a sudden surrounded by a\r
#Text=swarm of women and children, so that I thought it advisable to\r
#Text=hasten back again to the ship's people--not that any one offered me\r
#Text=any violence; but they crowded round me, handled my dress, wanted to\r
#Text=put on my straw bonnet; and this familiarity was far from pleasant\r
#Text=on account of their extreme dirtiness.
#Text=The children seemed\r
#Text=shockingly neglected; many were covered with pimples and small\r
#Text=sores; and both great and small had their hands constantly in their\r
#Text=hair.
#Text=At the places where we stopped they generally brought sheep and\r
#Text=butter, both of which were singularly cheap.
#Text=A sheep cost at the\r
#Text=utmost five krans (4s. 6d.).
#Text=They were very large and fat, with\r
#Text=long thick wool, and fat tails of about fifteen inches long and\r
#Text=eight inches broad.
#Text=Our crew had a better diet than I had ever\r
#Text=noticed on board any ship.
#Text=What pleased me even more was the equal\r
#Text=good treatment of the natives, who were not in any particular less\r
#Text=thought of than the English.
#Text=I never met with greater order and\r
#Text=cleanliness than here--a proof that blows and thumps are not\r
#Text=indispensably necessary, as I had so often been assured.
#Text=In the districts where the ground was covered with underwood and\r
#Text=grass, I saw several herds of wild swine; and there were said to be\r
#Text=lions here, who come from the mountains, especially during the\r
#Text=winter time, when they carried off cows and sheep:  they very seldom\r
#Text=attacked men.
#Text=I was so fortunate as to see a pair of lions, but at\r
#Text=such a distance, that I cannot say whether they exceeded in beauty\r
#Text=and size those in European menageries.
#Text=Among the birds, the\r
#Text=pelicans were so polite as to make their respects to us by scraping.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=21st May.
#Text=Today we saw the ruins of the palace of Khuszew\r
#Text=Anushirwan at Ctesiphon.
#Text=Ctesiphon was formerly the capital of the\r
#Text=Parthian, and afterwards of the new Persian empire:  it was\r
#Text=destroyed by the Arabs in the seventeenth century.
#Text=Nearly opposite,\r
#Text=on the right bank of the Tigris, lay Seleucia, one of the most\r
#Text=celebrated towns of Babylon, and which, at the time of its\r
#Text=prosperity, had a free independent government and a population of\r
#Text=600,000 souls.
#Text=The chief portion were Greeks.
#Text=One obtained two views of Ctesiphon in passing, in consequence of\r
#Text=the river winding considerably--almost running back again several\r
#Text=miles.
#Text=I made a trip there from Baghdad, and therefore reserve my\r
#Text=account of it.
#Text=The old caliphate appears in marvellous magnificence and extent from\r
#Text=a distance, but unfortunately loses this on nearer approach.
#Text=The\r
#Text=minarets and cupolas, inlaid with variegated earthenware tiles,\r
#Text=glitter in the clear sunlight; palaces, gateways, and fortified\r
#Text=works, in endless succession, bound the yellow, muddy Tigris; and\r
#Text=gardens, with date and other fruit trees, cover the flat country for\r
#Text=miles round.
#Text=We had scarcely anchored, when a number of natives surrounded the\r
#Text=ship.
#Text=They made use of very singular vehicles, which resemble round\r
#Text=baskets:  these are formed of thick palm leaves, and covered with\r
#Text=asphalt.
#Text=They are called "guffer;" are six feet in diameter and\r
#Text=three feet in height; are very safe, for they never upset, and may\r
#Text=be travelled in over the worst roads.
#Text=Their invention is very\r
#Text=ancient.
#Text=I had a letter to the English resident, Major Rawlinson; but as Mr.
#Text=Holland, the first officer of the ship, offered me the use of his\r
#Text=house, I took advantage of this, on account of his being a married\r
#Text=man, which Mr.
#Text=Rawlinson was not.
#Text=I found Mrs.
#Text=Holland a very\r
#Text=pretty, amiable woman (a native of Baghdad), who, though only three-\r
#Text=and-twenty, had already four children, the eldest of whom was eight\r
#Text=years old.
#Text=CHAPTER XVIII.
#Text=MESOPOTAMIA, BAGHDAD, AND BABYLON.
#Text=BAGHDAD--PRINCIPAL BUILDINGS--CLIMATE--ENTERTAINMENT AT THE ENGLISH\r
#Text=RESIDENT'S--HAREM OF THE PASCHA OF BAGHDAD--EXCURSION TO THE RUINS\r
#Text=OF CTESIPHON--THE PERSIAN PRINCE, IL-HANY-ALA-CULY-MIRZA--EXCURSION\r
#Text=TO THE RUINS OF BABYLON--DEPARTURR FROM BAGHDAD.
#Text=Baghdad, the capital of Assyria, was founded during the reign of the\r
#Text=Caliph Abu-Jasar-Almansor.
#Text=A century later, in the reign of Haroun-\r
#Text=al-Raschid, the best and most enlightened of all the caliphs, the\r
#Text=town was at its highest pitch of prosperity; but at the end of\r
#Text=another century, it was destroyed by the Turks.
#Text=In the sixteenth\r
#Text=century it was conquered by the Persians, and continued to be a\r
#Text=perpetual source of discord between them and the Turks, although it\r
#Text=at length became annexed to the Ottoman Empire.
#Text=Nadir Schah again\r
#Text=endeavoured to wrest it from the Turks in the eighteenth century.
#Text=The present population, of about 60,000 souls, consists of about\r
#Text=three-fourths Turks, and the remainder of Jews, Persians, Armenians,\r
#Text=and Arabs.
#Text=There are only fifty or sixty Europeans living there.
#Text=The town is partly situated on both sides of the Tigris, but chiefly\r
#Text=on the east.
#Text=It is surrounded by fortified walls of brick, with\r
#Text=numerous towers at regular intervals; both walls and towers,\r
#Text=however, are weak, and even somewhat dangerous, and the cannons upon\r
#Text=them are not in good condition.
#Text=The first thing that it was necessary for me to provide myself with\r
#Text=here, was a large linen wrapper, called isar, a small fez, and a\r
#Text=kerchief, which, wound round the fez, forms a little turban; but I\r
#Text=did not make use of the thick, stiff mask, made of horse-hair, which\r
#Text=covers the face, and under which the wearer is nearly suffocated.
#Text=It is impossible to imagine a more inconvenient out-door dress for\r
#Text=our sex than the one worn here.
#Text=The isar gathers the dust from the\r
#Text=ground, and it requires some dexterity to hold it together in such a\r
#Text=way as to envelop the whole body.
#Text=I pitied the poor women greatly,\r
#Text=who were often obliged to carry a child, or some other load, or\r
#Text=perhaps even to wash linen in the river.
#Text=They never came from this\r
#Text=work, except dripping with water.
#Text=Even the smallest girls here are\r
#Text=clothed in this way whenever they go out.
#Text=In my Oriental dress I could walk about without any covering on my\r
#Text=face, perfectly uninterrupted.
#Text=I first examined the town, but there\r
#Text=was not much to see, as there are no remains of the old Caliphate\r
#Text=buildings.
#Text=The houses are of burnt bricks, and are only one story\r
#Text=high; the backs are all turned towards the streets, and it is but\r
#Text=rarely that a projecting part of the house is seen with narrow\r
#Text=latticed windows.
#Text=Those houses only whose facades are towards the\r
#Text=Tigris make an exception to this rule; they have ordinary windows,\r
#Text=and are sometimes very handsome.
#Text=I found the streets rather narrow,\r
#Text=and full of dirt and dust.
#Text=The bridge of boats over the Tigris,\r
#Text=which is here 690 feet broad, is the most wretched that I ever saw.
#Text=The bazaars are very extensive.
#Text=The old bazaar, a relic of the\r
#Text=former town, still shows traces of handsome columns and arabesques,\r
#Text=and Chan Osman is distinguished by its beautiful portal and lofty\r
#Text=arches.
#Text=The principal passages are so broad, that there is room for\r
#Text=a horseman and two foot passengers, to go through side by side.
#Text=The\r
#Text=merchants and artisans here, as in all eastern countries, live in\r
#Text=separate streets and passages.
#Text=The better shops are to be found in\r
#Text=private houses, or in the chans at the bazaars.
#Text=Miserable coffee-\r
#Text=stalls are everywhere numerous.
#Text=The palace of the pascha is an extensive building, but neither\r
#Text=tasteful nor costly; it is imposing only from a distance.
#Text=There are\r
#Text=but few mosques, and those present nothing costly or artistic,\r
#Text=except the inlaid tiles.
#Text=To be able to overlook the whole of Baghdad, I mounted, with great\r
#Text=difficulty, the exterior of the dome of the Osman Chan, and was\r
#Text=truly astounded at the extent and beautiful position of the town.
#Text=It is impossible to form any idea of an Oriental town by passing\r
#Text=through the narrow and uniform streets, no matter how often, as\r
#Text=these are all alike, and, one with the other, resemble the passages\r
#Text=of a jail.
#Text=But, from above, I looked down over the whole town, with\r
#Text=its innumerable houses, many of which are situated in pretty\r
#Text=gardens.
#Text=I saw thousands and thousands of terraces spread at my\r
#Text=feet, and before all, the beautiful river, rolling on through dark\r
#Text=orchards and palm groves, to the town, which extends along its banks\r
#Text=for five miles.
#Text=All the buildings are, as already remarked, constructed of unburnt\r
#Text=bricks, of which the greater part are stated to have been brought\r
#Text=down the Euphrates, from the ruins of the neighbouring city of\r
#Text=Babylon.
#Text=By a close examination, traces of the old architecture are\r
#Text=to be found on the fortifications; the bricks of which they are\r
#Text=built are about two feet in diameter, and resemble fine slabs of\r
#Text=stone.
#Text=The houses are prettier inside than out; they have clean plastered\r
#Text=courts, numerous windows, etc.
#Text=The rooms are large and lofty, but\r
#Text=not nearly so magnificently furnished as those in Damascus.
#Text=The\r
#Text=summer is so hot here, that people find it necessary to change their\r
#Text=rooms three times a-day.
#Text=The early part of the morning is passed in\r
#Text=the ordinary rooms; towards 9 o'clock they retire, during the\r
#Text=remainder of the day, into the underground rooms, called sardab,\r
#Text=which, like cellars, are frequently situated fifteen or twenty feet\r
#Text=below the surface; at sunset they go up on to the terraces, where\r
#Text=they receive visits, gossip, drink tea, and remain until night.
#Text=This is the most pleasant time, as the evenings are cool and\r
#Text=enlivening.
#Text=Many affirm the moonlight is clearer here than with us,\r
#Text=but I did not find this to be the case.
#Text=People sleep on the\r
#Text=terraces under mosquito nets, which surround the whole bed.
#Text=The\r
#Text=heat rises in the rooms, during the day, as high as 99 degrees; in\r
#Text=the sun, to 122 or 131 degrees Fah.; it seldom exceeds 88 degrees\r
#Text=25' in the sardabs.
#Text=In winter, the evenings, nights, and mornings\r
#Text=are so cold, that fires are necessary in the rooms.
#Text=The climate of this place is considered very healthy, even by\r
#Text=Europeans.
#Text=Nevertheless, there is a disease here of which the young\r
#Text=females are terribly afraid, and which not only attacks the natives,\r
#Text=but strangers, when they remain several months here.
#Text=This is a\r
#Text=disgusting eruption, which is called the Aleppo Boil, or Date-mark.
#Text=This ulcer, which is at first no larger than a pin's head, gradually\r
#Text=increases to the size of a halfcrown piece, and leaves deep scars.
#Text=It generally breaks out on the face; there is scarcely one face\r
#Text=among a hundred, to be seen without these disfiguring marks.
#Text=Those\r
#Text=who have only one have reason to consider themselves fortunate; I\r
#Text=saw many with two or three of them.
#Text=Other parts of the body are\r
#Text=also not exempt.
#Text=The ulcers generally appear with the ripening of\r
#Text=the dates, and do not go away until the next year, when the same\r
#Text=season returns again.
#Text=This disease does not occur more than once in\r
#Text=a lifetime; it attacks children for the most part during their\r
#Text=infancy.
#Text=No remedy is ever applied, as experience has shown that it\r
#Text=cannot be prevented; the Europeans have tried inoculation, but\r
#Text=without success.
#Text=This disease is met with in several districts on the Tigris; there\r
#Text=are no traces of it to be found at a distance from the river.
#Text=It\r
#Text=would appear, therefore, to be, in some way, connected with the\r
#Text=evaporation from the stream, or the mud deposited on its banks; the\r
#Text=former seems less probable, as the crews of the English steamers,\r
#Text=which are always on the river, escape, while all the Europeans who\r
#Text=live on land fall victims to it.
#Text=One of the latter had forty such\r
#Text=boils, and I was told that he suffered horribly.
#Text=The French consul,\r
#Text=who expected to remain here for several years, would not bring his\r
#Text=wife with him, to expose her face to the danger of these\r
#Text=ineradicable marks.
#Text=I had only been here some weeks, when I\r
#Text=discovered slight indications of a boil on my hand, which became\r
#Text=large, but did not penetrate very deep, and left no permanent scar.
#Text=I exulted greatly at escaping so easily, but my exultation did not\r
#Text=continue long; only six months afterwards, when I had returned to\r
#Text=Europe, this disease broke out with such violence that I was covered\r
#Text=with thirteen of those boils, and had to contend with them more than\r
#Text=eight months.
#Text=On the 24th of May I received an invitation from the English\r
#Text=resident, Major Rawlinson, to an entertainment in honour of the\r
#Text=queen's birthday.
#Text=There were only Europeans present at dinner, but\r
#Text=in the evening, all denominations of the Christian world were\r
#Text=admitted--Armenians, Greeks, etc.
#Text=This entertainment was given upon\r
#Text=the handsome terraces of the house.
#Text=The floor was covered with soft\r
#Text=carpets; cushioned divans invited the fatigued to rest, and the\r
#Text=brilliant illumination of the terraces, courts, and gardens diffused\r
#Text=a light almost equal to that of day.
#Text=Refreshments of the most\r
#Text=delicate kind made it difficult for Europeans to remember that they\r
#Text=were so far from their native country.
#Text=Less deceptive were two\r
#Text=bands of music, one of which played European, the other native\r
#Text=pieces, for the amusement of the guests.
#Text=Fire-works, with balloons\r
#Text=and Bengal lights, were followed by a sumptuous supper, which closed\r
#Text=the evening's entertainments.
#Text=Among the women and girls present,\r
#Text=there were some remarkably beautiful, but all had most bewitching\r
#Text=eyes, which no young man could glance at with impunity.
#Text=The art of\r
#Text=dyeing the eyelids and eyebrows principally contributes to this.
#Text=Every hair on the eyebrows which makes its appearance in an improper\r
#Text=place, is carefully plucked out, and those which are deficient have\r
#Text=their place most artistically supplied by the pencil.
#Text=The most\r
#Text=beautiful arched form is thus obtained, and this, together with the\r
#Text=dyeing of the eyelids, increases uncommonly the brightness of the\r
#Text=eye.
#Text=The desire for such artificial beauty extends itself even to\r
#Text=the commonest servant girls.
#Text=The fair sex were dressed in Turkish-Greek costume; they wore silk\r
#Text=trousers, gathered together round the ankles, and over these, long\r
#Text=upper garments, embroidered with gold, the arms of which were tight\r
#Text=as far as the elbow, and were then slit open, and hung down.
#Text=The\r
#Text=bare part of the arm was covered by silk sleeves.
#Text=Round their\r
#Text=waists were fastened stiff girdles of the breadth of the hand,\r
#Text=ornamented in front with large buttons, and at the sides with\r
#Text=smaller ones.
#Text=The buttons were of gold, and worked in enamel.
#Text=Mounted pearls, precious stones, and gold coins, decorated the arms,\r
#Text=neck, and breast.
#Text=The head was covered with a small, pretty turban,\r
#Text=wound round with gold chains, or gold lace; numerous thin tresses of\r
#Text=hair stole from underneath, falling down to the hips.
#Text=Unfortunately, many of them had the bad taste to dye their hair, by\r
#Text=which its brilliant black was changed into an ugly brown-red.
#Text=Beautiful as this group of women were in appearance, their society\r
#Text=was very uninteresting, for an unbroken silence was maintained by\r
#Text=these members of our garrulous sex, and not one of their pretty\r
#Text=faces expressed an emotion or sentiment.
#Text=Mind and education, the\r
#Text=zests of life, were wanting.
#Text=The native girls are taught nothing;\r
#Text=their education is completed when they are able to read in their\r
#Text=mother tongue (Armenian or Arabian), and then, with the exception of\r
#Text=some religious books, they have no other reading.
#Text=It was more lively at a visit which I made, some days later, to the\r
#Text=harem of the pasha; there was then so much chatting, laughing, and\r
#Text=joking, that it was almost too much for me.
#Text=My visit had been\r
#Text=expected, and the women, fifteen in number, were sumptuously dressed\r
#Text=in the same way that I have already described; with the single\r
#Text=exception, that the upper garment (kaftan) was shorter, and made of\r
#Text=a more transparent material, and the turbans ornamented with ostrich\r
#Text=feathers.
#Text=I did not see any very handsome women here; they had only good eyes,\r
#Text=but neither noble nor expressive features.
#Text=The summer harem, in which I was received, was a pretty building, in\r
#Text=the most modern style of European architecture, with lofty, regular\r
#Text=windows.
#Text=It stood in the middle of a small flower-garden, which was\r
#Text=surrounded by a large fruit-garden.
#Text=After I had been here rather more than an hour, a table was laid,\r
#Text=and chairs placed round it.
#Text=The principal woman invited me to join\r
#Text=them, and leading the way, seated herself at the table, when,\r
#Text=without waiting till we were seated, she hastily picked out her\r
#Text=favourite morsels from the various dishes with her hands.
#Text=I was\r
#Text=also compelled to help myself with my hands, as there was no knife\r
#Text=and fork in the whole house, and it was only towards the end of the\r
#Text=meal that a large gold teaspoon was brought for me.
#Text=The table was profusely covered with excellent meat-dishes, with\r
#Text=different pilaus, and a quantity of sweet-meats and fruits.
#Text=I found\r
#Text=them all delicious, and one dish so much resembled our fritters,\r
#Text=that I almost thought it was meant for them.
#Text=After we had finished, those who had not room to sit down with us\r
#Text=took their seats together with some of the principal attendants:\r
#Text=after them came, in succession, the inferior slaves, among whom were\r
#Text=some very ugly negresses; these also seated themselves at the table,\r
#Text=and ate what remained.
#Text=After the conclusion of the meal, strong coffee was handed round in\r
#Text=small cups, and nargillies brought.
#Text=The cups stood in little golden\r
#Text=bowls, ornamented with pearls and turquoises.
#Text=The pasha's women are distinguished from their attendants and slaves\r
#Text=only by their dress and jewellery; in demeanour I found no\r
#Text=difference.
#Text=The attendants seated themselves without hesitation\r
#Text=upon the divans, joined, uninvited, in the conversation, smoked, and\r
#Text=drank coffee as we did.
#Text=Servants and slaves are far better and more\r
#Text=considerately treated by the natives than by the Europeans.
#Text=Only\r
#Text=the Turks hold slaves here.
#Text=During my search through the village, I noticed, from the number of\r
#Text=ruined houses and huts, that it seemed to have been of some extent\r
#Text=formerly.
#Text=Here, also, the last plague had carried off the greater\r
#Text=part of the inhabitants; for, at the present time, there were only a\r
#Text=few very poor families.
#Text=I here saw a very peculiar mode of making butter.
#Text=The cream was put\r
#Text=into a leathern bottle, and shaken about on the ground until the\r
#Text=butter had formed.
#Text=When made, it was put into another bottle filled\r
#Text=with water.
#Text=It was as white as snow, and I should have taken it for\r
#Text=lard if I had not seen it made.
#Text=I threw\r
#Text=my mantle down, and seating myself upon it, did not move from the\r
#Text=place, well knowing that a Mussulman never uses force towards a\r
#Text=woman, not even towards a Christian one.
#Text=And so it turned out; he\r
#Text=left me in my place and went grumbling away.
#Text=One of the pedlars\r
#Text=behaved himself in a very different manner:  when he saw that I had\r
#Text=nothing for my meal but dry bread, while he had cucumbers and sweet\r
#Text=melons, he gave me a cucumber and a melon, for which he would not\r
#Text=take any money.
#Text=The pilgrim also ate nothing else, although he had\r
#Text=only to send one of his servants to the village to procure either\r
#Text=fowls or eggs, etc.
#Text=The frugality of these people is really\r
#Text=astonishing.
#Text=The good man must have concluded that I had not tasted any food\r
#Text=today, for he very hospitably ordered breakfast immediately,\r
#Text=consisting of bread, sheep's cheese, and melons.
#Text=These were eaten\r
#Text=all together.
#Text=My hunger was so great that I found this plan\r
#Text=excellent.
#Text=I ate without ceasing.
#Text=The conversation, on the\r
#Text=contrary, was not so successful; my host did not understand any\r
#Text=European language, nor I any Asiatic language.
#Text=We made use of\r
#Text=signs, and I took pains to make him understand that I was desirous\r
#Text=of going on further as soon as possible.
#Text=He promised to do his\r
#Text=utmost for me, and also explained that he would see to me during my\r
#Text=stay; he was not married, and therefore could not receive me into\r
#Text=his own house, but would take me to one of his relations.
#Text=Mr.
#Text=Mansur was to the last very hospitable.
#Text=He not only gave me a\r
#Text=letter to a Persian living in Sauh-Bulak, but also provided me with\r
#Text=bread for the journey, some melons, cucumbers, and a small bottle of\r
#Text=sour milk.
#Text=The latter was particularly acceptable to me, and I\r
#Text=would advise every traveller to remember this nourishing and\r
#Text=refreshing drink.
#Text=Sour milk is put into a small bag of thick linen, the watery part\r
#Text=filters through, and the solid part can be taken out with a spoon,\r
#Text=and mixed with water as desired.
#Text=In the hot season, indeed, it\r
#Text=dries into cheese on the fourth or fifth day, but this also tastes\r
#Text=very well, and in four or five days you come to places where the\r
#Text=supply may be renewed.
#Text=I had arrived at Tebris at a rather unfavourable time--namely, the\r
#Text=fast month.
#Text=From sunrise to sunset nothing is eaten, nobody leaves\r
#Text=the house, there are neither visits nor company--indeed, nothing but\r
#Text=praying.
#Text=This ceremony is so strictly observed that invalids\r
#Text=frequently fall victims to it, as they will take neither medicine\r
#Text=nor food during the day; they believe that if they were to eat only\r
#Text=a mouthful, they would forfeit the salvation to be obtained by\r
#Text=fasting.
#Text=Many of the more enlightened make an exception to this\r
#Text=custom in cases of illness; however, in such an instance the\r
#Text=physician must send a written declaration to the priest, in which he\r
#Text=explains the necessity of taking medicine and food.
#Text=If the priest\r
#Text=puts his seal to this document, pardon is obtained.
#Text=I am not aware\r
#Text=whether this granting of indulgences was taken by the Mahomedans\r
#Text=from the Christians, or the reverse.
#Text=Girls are obliged to keep\r
#Text=these fasts after their tenth year, and boys after their fifteenth.
#Text=The sweet melons and peaches were just in their prime during my stay\r
#Text=at Tebris.
#Text=They were so delicious, that it may well be said Persia\r
#Text=is their native country.
#Text=The melons have more frequently a whitish,\r
#Text=or greenish, than a yellow pulp.
#Text=They may be eaten entirely, with\r
#Text=the exception of the outermost thin rind; and, if it were possible\r
#Text=for anything to exceed sugar in sweetness, it would be these melons.
#Text=The peaches are also juicy, sweet, and aromatic.
#Text=The little town of Redutkale may contain about 1,500 inhabitants.
#Text=The men are so indolent that, during the five days that I passed\r
#Text=here, I could not procure a few grapes or figs for love or money.
#Text=I\r
#Text=went daily to the bazaar, and never found any for sale.
#Text=The people\r
#Text=are too lazy to bring wood from the forest; they work only when the\r
#Text=greatest necessity compels them, and require to be paid\r
#Text=exorbitantly.
#Text=I paid as much, if not more, for eggs, milk, and\r
#Text=bread as I would have done in Vienna.
#Text=It might well be said that\r
#Text=the people are here in the midst of plenty, and yet almost starve.
#Text=I was not better pleased by the thoughtless and meaningless\r
#Text=performance of religious ceremonies among these people.
#Text=On all\r
#Text=occasions, they cross themselves before eating or drinking, before\r
#Text=entering a room, before putting on an article of clothing, etc.
#Text=The\r
#Text=hands have nothing else to do but to make crosses.
#Text=But the most\r
#Text=provoking thing of all is, that they stand still before every church\r
#Text=they pass, bow half a dozen times, and cross themselves without end.
#Text=When they are travelling, they stop their carriages to perform this\r
#Text=ceremony.
